content.text: saves parts of your prompt so Claude doesn't have to reprocess them every API call. Instead of rebuilding the model's internal "attention states" (how words connect) from scratch each time, cached sections are reused—making responses

API Call 1: Prompt → Build attention map → Response
API Call 2: Same Prompt → Build attention map AGAIN → Response

API Call 1: Prompt → Build attention map → Cache stored → Response
API Call 2: Same Prompt → Use cached map → Response (no recalculation)
The system checks if a cached version exists. If yes, it's reused. If not, the full prompt is processed and stored.
Up to 90% reduction on repeated prompts
No need to recalculate attention states from scratch
Repeated questions, FAQs, long multi-turn conversations, large document retrieval

Most helpful when dealing with:
- Long prompts with many examples
- Big data chunks from vector databases
- Multi-turn conversations needing memory of previous instructions
Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Claude 3 Opus, Claude 3 Haiku

No manual reset needed; clears automatically

Understanding the internals of what is prompt caching helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with what is prompt caching has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying what is prompt caching in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

What Is Prompt Caching is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
The Problem That Prompt Caching Solves

Every Anthropic API call processes all input tokens from scratch: the system prompt, conversation history, documents, and user message are all tokenized and processed through the model's attention layers even when most of that content is identical across requests. For applications that repeatedly send large, static context — a 50,000-token legal document, a codebase embedded as context, or a 10,000-token system prompt with extensive instructions — this redundant processing constitutes the majority of both cost and latency. Prompt caching eliminates this redundancy by storing processed token computations server-side and reusing them on subsequent requests that contain the same prefix. The result is dramatically lower cost and faster time-to-first-token for cache-hit requests. This matters most for document-heavy workflows (retrieval-augmented generation, legal analysis, code review), long-running conversations, and any application where the same large context block appears in many requests.
Without caching: every API call reprocesses the full input token count, even if 90% of tokens are identical to the previous call.
With caching: identical prefix tokens are reused from a server-side store, reducing both cost and latency for cache hits.
Highest benefit for: large static documents, long system prompts, few-shot examples, and multi-turn conversations with growing history.
Cache hits are dramatically faster: TTFT (time-to-first-token) drops significantly because the model skips reprocessing cached tokens.
Caching is complementary to other optimizations — it works alongside streaming, tool use, and extended thinking.
Not all content benefits equally: short, frequently-changing content has low cache hit rates and may not justify the write cost.
How Cache Control Works in the API

Prompt caching is activated by adding a cache_control parameter to content blocks in the messages array or system prompt. The cache_control object has a single field: type, which must be set to "ephemeral" — the only supported cache type. When you mark a content block with cache_control: {type: "ephemeral"}, Anthropic's servers process that content and store the resulting KV (key-value) cache state for five minutes. Any subsequent request that includes the same content block with the same cache_control marker and the same preceding context will be served from cache. The cache key is computed from the full prefix of tokens up to and including the cached block — if any earlier token changes, the cache key changes and a cache miss occurs. This means your cacheable content should be placed early in the context, before any variable content such as the user's current message.
Activate by adding cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"} to a content block in the messages array or system prompt.
Cache TTL: 5 minutes from the last cache write; each cache hit resets the TTL, keeping frequently-used caches alive.
Cache key: computed from all tokens from the start of the request up to and including the cached block.
Ordering matters: place cacheable static content (system prompt, documents) before variable content (user message) to maximize cache hits.
Minimum cacheable size: 1024 tokens for Claude 3 models; smaller content blocks cannot be cached.
Maximum cache breakpoints: up to 4 cache_control markers per request, allowing layered caching of multiple large blocks.

Understanding cache pricing is essential for calculating whether caching is cost-effective for your workload. Cache writes (the first request that stores content in the cache) cost 25% more than standard input token pricing — you pay a premium on the first request to fund the storage. Cache reads (subsequent requests that hit the cache) cost approximately 90% less than standard input token pricing — a massive discount that offsets the write premium after just two cache hits. For content cached five times, the blended cost is: 1.25× (write) + 4× 0.10× (reads) = 0.65× the cost of five uncached requests. For a workload with a 10:1 read-to-write ratio, prompt caching reduces cost by over 85% on the cached portion of input tokens. The break-even point is approximately 1.25/(1 - 0.10) ≈ 1.4 cache reads per write; anything above that is net savings.
Cache write cost: 1.25× standard input token price (25% surcharge for the first request that stores to cache).
Cache read cost: 0.10× standard input token price (90% discount on cache hit requests).
Break-even: approximately 2 cache reads per write — reached quickly for any content queried more than twice.
Maximum savings: at high read-to-write ratios (100:1), cost approaches 0.10× of what uncached requests would cost.
Track cache efficiency using usage.cache_read_input_tokens and usage.cache_creation_input_tokens in API responses.

Not all content in a request is eligible for caching. The minimum cacheable block size is 1024 tokens — content shorter than this cannot carry a cache_control marker because the overhead of cache management exceeds the savings. In practice, this means short system prompts (under ~750 words) do not benefit from caching at all. Content that changes on every request (the user's current message, a timestamp, a request ID) should never be marked cacheable because it will always result in a cache miss and you will pay the write premium for no benefit. The ideal candidates for caching are: long static system prompts with extensive instructions or domain knowledge, large reference documents embedded in the context (legal contracts, codebases, manuals), and the stable portion of a growing conversation history. Few-shot examples are an excellent caching target: they are typically long, entirely static, and appear in every request.
Minimum size: 1024 tokens — content below this threshold cannot be cached regardless of the cache_control marker.
Best candidates: long system prompts (2000+ tokens), embedded documents, few-shot example sets, stable conversation history.
Never cache: user messages, timestamps, request IDs, or any content that is unique per request.
Few-shot examples: ideal for caching — static, long, and appear in every request in high-volume pipelines.
Conversation history: cache the stable prefix of a long conversation and append the current exchange as uncached content.
Versioned content: when document content changes (new version of a manual), the cache key changes automatically — no manual invalidation needed.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# A long reference document (must be 1024+ tokens to benefit from caching)
LEGAL_DOCUMENT = """
MASTER SERVICE AGREEMENT

1. DEFINITIONS
   1.1 "Agreement" means this Master Service Agreement including all exhibits.
   1.2 "Services" means the software services described in each Order Form.
   1.3 "Customer Data" means all data submitted by Customer to the Services.
   ... (imagine 50,000 tokens of actual document content here) ...
""" * 50  # Simulate a large document for demonstration

def analyze_document(question: str) -> dict:
    """Ask a question about the document, using prompt caching."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": "You are a legal document analyst. Answer questions about the provided document accurately.",
                # Cache the system prompt instructions
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
            }
        ],
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": [
                    {
                        "type": "text",
                        "text": f"Document:\n{LEGAL_DOCUMENT}",
                        # Cache the large document — this is the main cost savings
                        "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
                    },
                    {
                        "type": "text",
                        # User question is NOT cached — it changes per request
                        "text": f"Question: {question}"
                    }
                ]
            }
        ]
    )

    usage = response.usage
    return {
        "answer": response.content[0].text,
        "input_tokens": usage.input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": usage.output_tokens,
        "cache_creation_tokens": getattr(usage, "cache_creation_input_tokens", 0),
        "cache_read_tokens": getattr(usage, "cache_read_input_tokens", 0),
    }

# First call: cache miss — pays write premium, populates cache
result1 = analyze_document("What is the definition of Customer Data?")
print(f"Cache write tokens: {result1['cache_creation_tokens']}")
print(f"Answer: {result1['answer'][:100]}...")

# Second call: cache hit — 90% cheaper on cached tokens
result2 = analyze_document("What services are covered by this agreement?")
print(f"Cache read tokens: {result2['cache_read_tokens']}")
print(f"Answer: {result2['answer'][:100]}...")
